planting.note: Sow fresh, ripe seed in fall directly in a shaded woodland bed; seed requires a period of cold, moist conditioning to break dormancy and will germinate the following spring. Lenten rose is a slow-maturing perennial that blooms for weeks in late winter and early spring, producing nodding flowers in shades of white, pink, rose, plum, and near-black before most other shade perennials emerge.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial to full shade; seed loses viability quickly when dried, so sow as soon as possible after harvest and allow natural winter cold to break dormancy. Helleborus orientalis is a tough, long-lived evergreen perennial native to Greece and Turkey that thrives under deciduous trees and at the base of north-facing slopes, producing leathery, deeply divided basal leaves and nodding, cup-shaped flowers in late winter to early spring — one of the earliest perennial bloomers in the garden. Established clumps are drought-tolerant once the canopy closes in summer and are remarkably resistant to deer and rabbits; divide only if absolutely necessary, as plants resent disturbance. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested; wear gloves when handling, as the sap can irritate sensitive skin.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a woodland bed; seeds require cold, moist stratification over winter and germinate in early spring. Hairy sweet cicely is a native carrot-family herb of eastern North American forests with mild anise-scented foliage and lacy white umbel flowers that attract small native bees and flies in early spring.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; seed must not be allowed to dry out before sowing, as viability drops sharply with desiccation. Osmorhiza claytonii is one of the earliest-blooming native herbs in eastern deciduous forests, producing soft, fernlike, anise-scented foliage in early spring and flat-topped white umbels that serve as critical early nectar sources for small native bees and beneficial insects before most woodland flowers open. The plant naturalizes readily by self-seeding in moist shaded areas and is a useful addition to native woodland gardens, forest edges, and streamside plantings. Roots and young leaves carry a mild, pleasant anise flavor and have a long history of food and medicinal use by Indigenous peoples of eastern North America.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in cool, moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; mountain wood sorrel is a delicate native groundcover of cool northern and high-elevation forests that requires consistently moist, cool conditions and will struggle in hot, dry summers.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in cool, consistently moist, acidic, humus-rich soil — pH 4.5 to 5.5 — in partial to deep shade; mountain wood sorrel does not tolerate heat or drought and is best suited to USDA zones 2 through 6, where cool summers prevail. Oxalis montana is a charming, low-growing native groundcover of boreal and montane forests from the Appalachians north through Canada, spreading slowly by slender rhizomes to form a soft carpet of clover-like trifoliate leaves that fold downward at night and in response to intense light. In late spring, solitary white flowers with distinctive pink veining appear just above the foliage, creating a delicate, painterly effect on the forest floor. This plant is an excellent groundcover for shaded rock gardens, cool ravines, and the north-facing slopes where few other groundcovers thrive.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a shaded woodland bed so that winter cold provides the stratification needed for spring germination; Carolina spring beauty is a spring ephemeral that goes completely dormant by late spring, so mark planting locations carefully. Combine with later-emerging shade perennials like ferns or wild ginger to maintain interest after the foliage vanishes.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade under deciduous trees; seeds require cold, moist stratification and will germinate in early spring before the tree canopy leafs out. Claytonia caroliniana is a delicate spring ephemeral of rich hardwood forests in eastern North America, closely related to Virginia spring beauty (C. virginica) but distinguished by its broader, oval leaves; it produces loose racemes of white to pale pink flowers with darker pink veins in early spring, providing one of the earliest nectar sources for queen bumblebees and early native bees emerging from overwintering. The plant grows from a small, deep-seated corm and goes completely dormant by late May or June; the corms are edible and have a mild, chestnut-like flavor, though wild plants should never be harvested. Plant densely in drifts of 25 or more for best visual impact on the spring woodland floor.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in cool, moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to deep shade; rosy twisted stalk is a slow-growing woodland perennial of cool northern and mountain forests that requires consistent moisture and cool summer temperatures.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in cool, consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; established plants spread slowly by rhizomes and form loose colonies over many years without becoming invasive. Streptopus roseus is a graceful, Solomon's-seal-like woodland perennial of boreal and montane forests across northern and eastern North America, producing arching stems 12 to 24 inches tall with broad, sessile, parallel-veined leaves; uniquely, its tiny rose-pink, bell-shaped flowers hang singly beneath each leaf from a distinctly kinked or twisted flower stalk — the characteristic that gives the plant its common name. Small red berries follow the flowers in late summer and are eagerly eaten by forest birds, though they are considered mildly toxic to humans. This is a plant for patient gardeners: seed exhibits double dormancy requiring two cold periods before germination, making division the practical propagation method for most gardeners.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a shaded, moist woodland bed; seeds benefit from cold stratification over winter and germinate in spring. American lovage is a tall, celery-scented native herb of rich, moist eastern woodlands whose white umbel flowers attract native bees, flies, and wasps from late spring through summer.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade; cold stratification over winter improves germination rates significantly, and established plants will self-sow once the colony matures. Ligusticum canadense is a robust native carrot-family herb of rich, moist deciduous and mixed forests across the central and southern Appalachians and adjacent piedmont, growing 2 to 4 feet tall with large, finely divided, celery-scented leaves and broad, flat-topped white umbel flowers in late spring and early summer that are among the most effective native plants for attracting small native bees, parasitoid wasps, and syrphid flies. The aromatic roots and foliage were used extensively in food and medicine by many Indigenous peoples throughout the plant's range, and the plant serves as a larval host for the black swallowtail butterfly. It is an excellent structural plant for the back of shaded borders and native woodland gardens where height and late-season structure are needed.
